title: Chapter Twenty-Three: Nadine's Truth
content: The siege moaned against the walls like something hungry, and the painting pulled at me from the other room, a fishline through my sternum, but I went to my grandmother instead.

Not to heal a ghost. Not to burn a hex or seal a threshold or any of the things I'd learned from journals written in dead women's hands. I went because there was a wound in that chair by the window and it was still breathing, and I needed to understand it before I could hope to survive my own.

The rosemary was thick in Nadine's room. She'd kept a sprig on the windowsill for as long as I could remember — Mamá said it was for remembrance, but I think now it was for defiance. The scent tangled with something older beneath it: yellowed paper, dried ink, the faint mineral smell of books that had been read so many times their spines had cracked open like ribs. I pulled the chair beside hers and sat down.

She was smaller than I'd ever seen her. Not small the way I remembered from childhood, when she seemed like a quiet fixture of the house, a lamp left burning in the corner. Small the way a thing gets when something vital has been slowly removed. Her hands rested on the arms of the chair, her fingers like the pale roots of pulled-up flowers.
"Lola," I said.
Her eyes shifted toward me. Present. More present than they'd been in months.
"Tell me what happened to you."

I had asked the question before, in a hundred different ways, in a hundred different years of my life. But I had never asked it like this — not as a child bewildered by her silence, not as a girl secretly hoping she wouldn't answer. I asked it as the woman who needed the truth more than she needed to be spared from it. The bravest question I have ever asked.

Nadine looked at me for a long time. The siege groaned between the walls. A hex pattern I'd painted on the doorframe flickered amber and held. And then my grandmother opened her mouth and spoke — really spoke — in full sentences, a voice like paper but steady, so steady, as if she'd been holding these words for decades and they had calcified into something harder than weakness.
"I found the journals when I was nineteen."
I didn't move. I barely breathed.

"Hidden beneath the floorboards in Iris's old room. Seven of them. One for each woman before me." Her hands trembled on the chair's arms, just slightly. "I had three years before the curse activated. Three years to prepare. And I *did* prepare. I unlocked the gifts — Voice-Binding first, then the hex-work, then Iris's fire. I read every journal front to back. I memorized the patterns. I cross-referenced their failings. I was *going* to break it."

Her papery voice caught on the last word, and I heard something in it I had never heard before. Not fragility. *Fury.* She had been fierce. She had been ready. My grandmother — the woman who sat in the corner like furniture, who spoke in single words, who had to be reminded to eat — she had once been the most prepared Buena woman in history.
"I was twenty-two when he came," she said. "And he was beautiful."

She said it without sentiment. The way you state a fact about weather. *It rained. He was beautiful.* As if the beauty were a tactical detail, a weapon he carried, and she had catalogued it alongside everything else. But I heard what was underneath the flatness. I heard the echo of a younger woman who had not yet learned that beauty could be a doorway with teeth.

"And I fought." Her voice rose — not volume, but *texture*, like thin paper suddenly pulled taut. "I fought harder than any of them. I used Voice-Binding when he tried to persuade me. I used the hexes when he wouldn't leave. I used Iris's fire and I burned his portrait of me the moment I found it, burned it to ash on the first sitting. I did *everything* right, Aurelia. Everything the journals told me. Everything I'd spent three years learning."

She paused. The rosemary sweetened the air between us, and I could smell the oldness of the room, the decades of breath and solitude soaked into the walls. Somewhere below, a window cracked from the pressure of the siege, and I felt the building shudder, and then still.
"He told me he'd change."
Three words. And in them, an entire architecture of destruction.

"He told me he was different from the others. That he *saw* me. That what we had was *real*, not like the curse, not like the portraits — something separate, something we'd chosen." Her thin fingers curled against the chair. "And I wanted to believe him. I wanted it so badly that I *ate* the lie like it was bread. I was *starving* for it to be true. And the curse —"
She turned her head fully toward me, and her eyes, which I had only ever seen as fogged and distant, were *clear*. Painfully, punishingly clear.

"The curse knows what you're starving for. It doesn't override your desires. It *exploits* them. It doesn't make you want what destroys you. It finds what you already want — what you need, what you're hollow for — and it wraps itself around that hunger until you can't tell where your need ends and the curse begins. It feeds you *exactly* the thing that will keep you at the table, and you thank it for the meal."

I felt my throat close. Because I understood. I understood in my blood and my marrow and in the fire that lived beneath my ribs, the fire I'd been fighting to control since the painting started calling. I understood because I had felt the pull — not just Julian's pull, the painted compulsion, but the deeper, older want beneath it: the want to be *seen*, to be *chosen*, to be the one who mattered enough that someone would change for love of you. The curse knew that hunger. It had always known it. It had been feeding on Buena women for six generations.
Nadine's hands were shaking now, but her voice stayed steady, each word pressed flat like flowers in a book, preserved by the very pressure that had nearly destroyed them.

"I held out for three years. Three years of fighting and hexing and binding and burning and *refusing*. And then one night I was tired. That's all. I was just *tired*." She swallowed, and I watched her throat move like a bird's. "I went back to him at twenty-five. Everyone said I was strong. They said I'd be the one to break it. My mother said it. My aunts said it. The whole *town* said it, as if their expectations were armor I could wear instead of just another weight on my shoulders. But no one can be strong forever. The curse is *patient*. It waits until you're tired. Until you're lonely. Until the lie tastes better than the truth, and you're too wrung out to tell the difference."
She looked down at her hands. I looked at them too — at the knuckles, at the paper-thin skin, at the blue veins that mapped a geography of endurance.

"By thirty, he'd finished my portrait." Her voice dropped, but it did not crack. It was too dry to crack. "And I... emptied. Like a glass poured out. Like wine from a chalice — beautiful, and then gone, and only the shape left behind. The shape that used to be a person."

I wanted to reach for her then, but I didn't. Not yet. I needed to hear the rest. I needed to hear all of it, because she had never said any of this before, and I understood — with the terrible, lucid understanding that Iris's fire had given me — that this was not a confession. This was a *handoff*. She was giving me the truth because she could not carry it anymore, and if I didn't take it, it would die with her.
"I wasn't weak."
The rage hit me like a slap. Not directed at me — directed at the world, at the memory, at every person who had ever looked at her diminished and confused that wreckage for surrender.

"I was *exhausted*." Her voice was a blade now, thin but cutting. "Six women's wounds, passed down through blood and magic. Six generations of grief and betrayal and consumption, and they all lived in me — not metaphorically, not poetically, but *literally*. The curse carries forward what was done to each woman before. I held Iris's burns. I held Concepción's terror. I held Elena's shame. I held them in my body, in my blood, in the magic that turned against me every time I tried to use it, because the magic was *wounded* too. And I was supposed to carry all of them *alone*."
She turned toward me, and this time there were tears in her eyes, but they were fierce tears, angry tears, tears that had been dammed for forty years and were now eroding the walls that held them.

"No one helped me. No one said 'I'll carry some of this.' No one said 'You don't have to hold it all.' I was supposed to be strong — the one who would finally break it — and instead I was just *tired*. I was so tired, Aurelia. I was so tired that the thought of just... stopping... felt like mercy. And when you're that tired, surrender doesn't feel like defeat. It feels like *sleep*."

The room was very still. The siege had quieted — or maybe I had stopped hearing it. The rosemary smell was sharper now, almost medicinal, and beneath it the old-paper scent of journals that Nadine had read before I was born, before my mother was born, journals she had studied and annotated and wept over and *learned from*, so that one day her granddaughter could sit beside her and hear the truth she had never been able to speak aloud.

Because no one had asked. Not like this. Not with time running out and the walls coming down and the painting pulling with enough force to dislocate the soul. Not from a place of need rather than curiosity. No one had sat beside her and said *tell me what happened to you* and meant *I am ready to carry some of this with you.*
I reached over and took her hand.

Her fingers were thin — God, they were so thin, the bones like pick-up sticks beneath skin that felt like the tissue paper wrapped around old dresses. But she *gripped* me. She gripped my hand with a strength that her body should not have possessed, a strength that came from somewhere deeper than muscle, and I felt the tremor in her grip, the way her whole arm shook with the effort of holding on, and I held back. I held back with everything I had.
"You weren't weak," I said.
My voice was rough. Broken at the edges. But I made myself say it, because no one had ever said it to her, and she had needed to hear it for forty years.

"You were the first one to make it possible for me. Every journal you read — that was for me. Every gift you unlocked — that was for me. Every year you fought, every hex you cast, every night you lay awake and *refused*, even when you were so tired you could barely remember your own name — that was for *me*. You couldn't break it because one woman can't break what seven made. You were never supposed to break it alone. *No one* was."

Her grip on my hand tightened. I felt the bones shift under my fingers — hers and my own, pressing together through skin, two generations of women holding on against a current that had been drowning us for a hundred and sixty years.

Nadine's face changed. The distant, fogged expression that I had known my whole life — the glassy, half-there look of a woman who had checked out of her own existence — cracked open like ice on a river, and beneath it was a face I had never seen. Raw. Desperate. *Alive.*
She cried.

Not the papery, distant tears of a woman who had forgotten how to feel. Not the silent, automatic leakage of someone whose body had lost the script for grief. She cried the way a person cries when the wall finally comes down — ugly, heaving, gasping sobs that bent her forward in the chair, that shook the fragile architecture of her chest, that opened her mouth in a sound I had never heard from her and would never forget. A sound like someone surfacing after decades underwater, gulping air that burned on the way in because the lungs had forgotten how to expand.

I held her hand and she gripped it and the tears fell on our knuckles, on the arm of the chair, on the rosemary that had fallen from the windowsill, and I tasted salt on my own lips — mine or hers, I couldn't tell, and it didn't matter, because they were the same tears, the same ocean, the same water that had been held behind the same dam for six generations of women who had been told to be strong and had drowned in the telling.

The room smelled of rosemary and old paper and salt, and the siege had gone quiet as if even the darkness outside knew to shut its mouth, and my grandmother wept for the first time in decades, and the sound was the most terrible and the most beautiful thing I had ever heard.

When she finally lifted her head, her face was wrecked. Splotched and swollen and *visible* in a way it had never been. The distance was gone. The glass was gone. There was only Nadine — not the diminished echo of a woman, not the lamp in the corner, not the quiet fixture of my childhood. A person. A full, furious, devastated person who had carried an impossible burden and had not been allowed to put it down.
Her eyes found mine. Red-rimmed. Streaming. *Steady.*
"Then break it," she said.

Her voice was raw. Scraped clean of the papery distance, of the decades of silence, of the gentle vacancy that had protected her from feeling the full weight of what had been done. What remained was a voice that had been worn down to its core, and the core was *fire* — Iris's fire, Concepción's fire, Elena's fire, every woman's fire, passed forward through blood and magic and the stubborn, irrational refusal to let the flame go dark.
"Break it for all of us."

I held her hand. The siege shifted against the walls. The painting pulled. And somewhere in my chest, in the space where the fire lived, in the space where six generations of hunger and grief and love had been accumulating like winter snow on a branch that bends but does not break — somewhere in that space, something cracked open. Not breaking. *Beginning.*

I would not carry it alone either. That was the truth Nadine had given me — not the journals, not the hex patterns, not the gifts she'd unlocked with her fierce, terrified preparation. *This* truth. That the burden was never meant for one woman. That seven made the curse and it would take seven to unmake it. That no one saves you.
*We save us.*
I squeezed her hand, and she squeezed back, and the rosemary trembled on the windowsill, and I tasted salt, and I began to understand what breaking it would actually cost.
Not my strength. Not my will. Not my fire alone.
*All of us. Every wound. Every woman. Every generation of starvation turned to fuel.*
I had never been more afraid. I had never been more ready.
